(born **David Harold Meyer**; March 27, 1931 – February 13, 1980) was an American film and television actor who is best known for his starring role as [Richard Kimble](/wiki/Richard_Kimble "Richard Kimble") in the television series *[The Fugitive](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1963_TV_series) "The Fugitive (1963 TV series)")* (1963–1967). Janssen also had the title roles in three other series: *[Richard Diamond, Private Detective](/wiki/Richard_Diamond,_Private_Detective "Richard Diamond, Private Detective")*; *[O'Hara, U.S. Treasury](/wiki/O%27Hara,_U.S._Treasury "O'Hara, U.S. Treasury")*; and *[Harry O](/wiki/Harry_O "Harry O")*.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

American actor (1931–1980)

| David Janssen | |
| --- | --- |
| Janssen in *The Fugitive* (1963) | |
| Born | David Harold Meyer  (1931-03-27)March 27, 1931  [Naponee, Nebraska](/wiki/Naponee,_Nebraska "Naponee, Nebraska"), U.S. |
| Died | February 13, 1980(1980-02-13) (aged 48)  [Malibu, California](/wiki/Malibu,_California "Malibu, California"), U.S. |
| Resting place | [Hillside Memorial Park Cemetery](/wiki/Hillside_Memorial_Park_Cemetery "Hillside Memorial Park Cemetery") |
| Occupation | Actor |
| Years active | 1945–1980 |
| Spouses | Ellie Graham  ​  ​ (m. 1958; div. 1968)​      Dani Crayne  ​ (m. 1975)​ |

**David Janssen** (born **David Harold Meyer**; March 27, 1931 – February 13, 1980) was an American film and television actor who is best known for his starring role as [Richard Kimble](/wiki/Richard_Kimble "Richard Kimble") in the television series *[The Fugitive](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1963_TV_series) "The Fugitive (1963 TV series)")* (1963–1967). Janssen also had the title roles in three other series: *[Richard Diamond, Private Detective](/wiki/Richard_Diamond,_Private_Detective "Richard Diamond, Private Detective")*; *[O'Hara, U.S. Treasury](/wiki/O%27Hara,_U.S._Treasury "O'Hara, U.S. Treasury")*; and *[Harry O](/wiki/Harry_O "Harry O")*.

In 1996, *[TV Guide](/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide")* ranked him number 36 on its *50 Greatest TV Stars of All Time* list.[[1]](#cite_note-TV_Guide-1)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Janssen&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

David Janssen was born on March 27, 1931, in [Naponee, Nebraska](/wiki/Naponee,_Nebraska "Naponee, Nebraska"), as  **David Harold Meyer**. His father was Harold Edward Meyer, a banker, and his mother, Berniece Graf, was formerly Miss Nebraska and [a Ziegfeld girl](/wiki/Ziegfeld_girl "Ziegfeld girl").[[2]](#cite_note-2) Following his parents' divorce in 1935, his mother moved with David to Los Angeles and married Eugene Janssen in 1940. David identified with his Jewish stepfather and adopted his surname after entering show business as a child.[[3]](#cite_note-Hollywood_Walk_of_Fame_2019_v183-3)

Janssen attended [Fairfax High School](/wiki/Fairfax_High_School_(Los_Angeles) "Fairfax High School (Los Angeles)"), where he excelled on the basketball court, setting a school scoring record that lasted over 20 years. His first film part was at the age of thirteen, and by the age of twenty-five, he had appeared in twenty films and served two years as an enlisted man in the [United States Army](/wiki/United_States_Army "United States Army"). During his Army days, Janssen became a friend of fellow enlistees [Martin Milner](/wiki/Martin_Milner "Martin Milner") and [Clint Eastwood](/wiki/Clint_Eastwood "Clint Eastwood") while posted at [Fort Ord](/wiki/Fort_Ord "Fort Ord"), California.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

## Acting career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Janssen&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Acting career")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/David_Janssen "Special:EditPage/David Janssen") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *Find sources:* ["David Janssen"](https://www.google.com/search?as_eq=wikipedia&q=%22David+Janssen%22) – [news](https://www.google.com/search?tbm=nws&q=%22David+Janssen%22+-wikipedia&tbs=ar:1) **·** [newspapers](https://www.google.com/search?&q=%22David+Janssen%22&tbs=bkt:s&tbm=bks) **·** [books](https://www.google.com/search?tbs=bks:1&q=%22David+Janssen%22+-wikipedia) **·** [scholar](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=%22David+Janssen%22) **·** [JSTOR](https://www.jstor.org/action/doBasicSearch?Query=%22David+Janssen%22&acc=on&wc=on) *(May 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/David_Janssen_Harry_O_1975.JPG/250px-David_Janssen_Harry_O_1975.JPG)](/wiki/File:David_Janssen_Harry_O_1975.JPG)

Janssen in *Harry O* (1975)

Janssen starred in four television series of his own:

* *[Richard Diamond, Private Detective](/wiki/Richard_Diamond,_Private_Detective "Richard Diamond, Private Detective")* (1957–1960)[[6]](#cite_note-6)
* *[The Fugitive](/wiki/The_Fugitive_(1963_TV_series) "The Fugitive (1963 TV series)")* (1963–1967)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* *[O'Hara, U.S. Treasury](/wiki/O%27Hara,_U.S._Treasury "O'Hara, U.S. Treasury")* (1971–1972)[[8]](#cite_note-8)
* *[Harry O](/wiki/Harry_O "Harry O")* (1974–1976)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

At the time of its airing in August 1967, the final episode of *The Fugitive* held the record for the greatest number of American homes to watch a series finale – 72 percent.[[10]](#cite_note-10) In 1996 *[TV Guide](/wiki/TV_Guide "TV Guide")* ranked *The Fugitive* number 36 on its *50 Greatest Shows of All Time* list.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

His films include: *[To Hell and Back](/wiki/To_Hell_and_Back_(film) "To Hell and Back (film)")*, the biography of [Audie Murphy](/wiki/Audie_Murphy "Audie Murphy"), who was the most decorated American soldier of World War II; *[Hell to Eternity](/wiki/Hell_to_Eternity "Hell to Eternity")*, a 1960 American World War II biopic starring Jeffrey Hunter as a Hispanic boy who fought in the [Battle of Saipan](/wiki/Battle_of_Saipan "Battle of Saipan") and who was raised by Japanese-American foster parents; [John Wayne](/wiki/John_Wayne "John Wayne")'s Vietnam war film *[The Green Berets](/wiki/The_Green_Berets_(film) "The Green Berets (film)")*; opposite [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck"), in the space story *[Marooned](/wiki/Marooned_(1969_film) "Marooned (1969 film)")*, in which Janssen played an astronaut sent to rescue three stranded men in space; and *[The Shoes of the Fisherman](/wiki/The_Shoes_of_the_Fisherman_(movie) "The Shoes of the Fisherman (movie)")*, as a television journalist in Rome reporting on the election of a new Pope ([Anthony Quinn](/wiki/Anthony_Quinn "Anthony Quinn")).

He also played pilot Harry Walker in the 1973 action movie *[Birds of Prey](/wiki/Birds_of_Prey_(1973_film) "Birds of Prey (1973 film)")*. He starred as a Los Angeles police detective trying to clear himself in the killing of an apparently innocent doctor in the 1967 film *[Warning Shot](/wiki/Warning_Shot_(1967_film) "Warning Shot (1967 film)")*, which was shot during a break in the spring and summer of 1966 between the third and fourth seasons of *The Fugitive.*

Janssen played an alcoholic in the 1977 TV movie *A Sensitive, Passionate Man*, which co-starred [Angie Dickinson](/wiki/Angie_Dickinson "Angie Dickinson"), and played an engineer who devises an unbeatable system for [blackjack](/wiki/Blackjack "Blackjack") in the 1978 made-for-TV movie *Nowhere to Run*, co-starring [Stefanie Powers](/wiki/Stefanie_Powers "Stefanie Powers") and [Linda Evans](/wiki/Linda_Evans "Linda Evans"). Janssen's impressively husky voice was used to good effect as the narrator for the TV mini-series *Centennial* (1978–79); he also appeared in the final episode. And in 1979 he starred in the made-for-TV mini series *S.O.S. Titanic* as John Jacob Astor, playing opposite Beverly Ross as his wife, Madeleine.

Though Janssen's scenes were cut from the final release, he also appeared as a journalist in the film *[Inchon](/wiki/Inchon_(film) "Inchon (film)")*, which he accepted to work with [Laurence Olivier](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier "Laurence Olivier"), who played General [Douglas MacArthur](/wiki/Douglas_MacArthur "Douglas MacArthur"). At the time of his death, Janssen had just begun filming a television movie playing the part of [Father Damien](/wiki/Father_Damien "Father Damien"), the priest who dedicated himself to the [leper](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy") colony on the island of [Molokai](/wiki/Molokai "Molokai"), Hawaii. The part was eventually reassigned to actor [Ken Howard](/wiki/Ken_Howard "Ken Howard") of the CBS series *[The White Shadow](/wiki/The_White_Shadow_(TV_series) "The White Shadow (TV series)")*.

* [![David Janssen in 1957](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/29/David_Janssen_Richard_Diamond_1957.JPG/250px-David_Janssen_Richard_Diamond_1957.JPG)](/wiki/File:David_Janssen_Richard_Diamond_1957.JPG "David Janssen in 1957")

  David Janssen in 1957
* [![Janssen as Richard Diamond (1959)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a0/David_Janssen_Richard_Diamond_1959.JPG/250px-David_Janssen_Richard_Diamond_1959.JPG)](/wiki/File:David_Janssen_Richard_Diamond_1959.JPG "Janssen as Richard Diamond (1959)")

  Janssen as Richard Diamond (1959)
* [![David Janssen as Dr. Richard Kimble in the TV series The Fugitive, 1967 (final episode)](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/David_Janssen_Fugitive_1967.JPG/250px-David_Janssen_Fugitive_1967.JPG)](/wiki/File:David_Janssen_Fugitive_1967.JPG "David Janssen as Dr. Richard Kimble in the TV series The Fugitive, 1967 (final episode)")

  David Janssen as Dr. Richard Kimble in the TV series *The Fugitive*, 1967 (final episode)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Janssen&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/David_Janssen_1974.JPG/250px-David_Janssen_1974.JPG)](/wiki/File:David_Janssen_1974.JPG)

in 1974

Janssen was married twice, first to interior decorator Ellie Graham from 1958 until their divorce in 1968.[[12]](#cite_note-12)[[13]](#cite_note-arar-13) In 1964, amid marital problems with Ellie, Janssen had a three-month affair with [Suzanne Pleshette](/wiki/Suzanne_Pleshette "Suzanne Pleshette").[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-15) In 1975, he married actress-turned-socialite [Dani Crayne](/wiki/Dani_Crayne "Dani Crayne"), and they remained married until Janssen's death in 1980.[[16]](#cite_note-gliatto-16)

## Death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=David_Janssen&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Death")]

Janssen was a heavy drinker, and a [chain smoker](/wiki/Chain_smoker "Chain smoker") who smoked up to four packs of cigarettes a day.[[17]](#cite_note-17) He died from a sudden heart attack in the early morning of February 13, 1980, at his beachfront home in [Malibu, California](/wiki/Malibu,_California "Malibu, California"), at the age of 48.[[13]](#cite_note-arar-13)[[18]](#cite_note-18) At the time of his death, Janssen was filming the television movie *Father Damien*. Janssen was buried at the [Hillside Memorial Park Cemetery](/wiki/Hillside_Memorial_Park_Cemetery "Hillside Memorial Park Cemetery") in [Culver City, California](/wiki/Culver_City,_California "Culver City, California").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

A non-denominational funeral was held at the Jewish chapel of the cemetery on February 17. [Suzanne Pleshette](/wiki/Suzanne_Pleshette "Suzanne Pleshette") delivered the eulogy at the request of Janssen's widow. [Milton Berle](/wiki/Milton_Berle "Milton Berle"), [Johnny Carson](/wiki/Johnny_Carson "Johnny Carson"), Tommy Gallagher, [Richard Harris](/wiki/Richard_Harris "Richard Harris"), [Carl Weathers](/wiki/Carl_Weathers "Carl Weathers"), [Stan Herman](/wiki/Stan_Herman "Stan Herman"), [Rod Stewart](/wiki/Rod_Stewart "Rod Stewart"), and [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck") were among Janssen's pallbearers. Honorary pallbearers included [Jack Lemmon](/wiki/Jack_Lemmon "Jack Lemmon"), [George Peppard](/wiki/George_Peppard "George Peppard"), [James Stewart](/wiki/James_Stewart "James Stewart"), and [Danny Thomas](/wiki/Danny_Thomas "Danny Thomas").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

For his contribution to the television industry, David Janssen has a star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame located on the 7700 block of [Hollywood Boulevard](/wiki/Hollywood_Boulevard "Hollywood Boulevard").[[22]](#cite_note-22)
